name: Trivy tag poisoning
description: An attacker force-pushed a malicious commit to a Trivy release tag, poisoning consumers that pinned only to the mutable tag instead of an immutable commit SHA.
detectionSignals: Workflow uses third-party actions by tag instead of commit SHA
Security-sensitive actions are not pinned to immutable digests
No policy enforcement for immutable action references

name: tj-actions/changed-files (Shai-Hulud) token exfiltration
description: A compromised GitHub Action release exfiltrated CI secrets through workflow logs, enabling unauthorized reuse of tokens and secrets in downstream environments.
detectionSignals: Use of affected action versions
Workflows expose secrets to untrusted execution contexts
CI logs include sensitive values or token-like patterns

name: actions-cool/issues-helper tag hijack
description: The actions-cool/issues-helper action repository was compromised and all tags were moved to an imposter commit outside normal branch history. The malicious payload downloaded Bun, attempted to read GitHub Actions runner process memory to harvest decrypted secrets, and exfiltrated credentials to attacker infrastructure.
detectionSignals: Workflow references actions-cool/issues-helper by mutable tag instead of immutable full commit SHA
Action tags resolve to an imposter commit not reachable from legitimate branch history
Unexpected outbound HTTPS traffic from CI after third-party action execution
No egress block policy to prevent credential exfiltration from runners

name: nx/Pwn Request
description: A pull request trigger abuse pattern allowed attacker-controlled code to execute in CI with elevated repository context, enabling tampering and secret access.
detectionSignals: Unsafe use of pull_request_target with checkout of untrusted code
Write-capable tokens available to PR-triggered workflows
No workflow isolation for fork-originated pull requests

name: Axios npm token leak
description: An npm publish token was exposed in CI logs, allowing an attacker to publish malicious package versions under a trusted package name.
detectionSignals: CI pipelines print environment variables or token-containing commands
Long-lived npm tokens used instead of scoped automation tokens
No log redaction or secret masking validation

name: Codecov bash uploader supply chain compromise
description: Attackers gained access to Codecov's CI environment and modified the bash uploader script to exfiltrate environment variables — including repository secrets and tokens — from any CI pipeline that downloaded and executed the script. Thousands of organizations were affected before discovery.
detectionSignals: Default branch allows force pushes with no branch protection
No required reviewers on default branch
CI artifacts or scripts fetched without integrity verification

name: Uber credential exposure via repository secret
description: An attacker obtained valid credentials through social engineering and discovered additional high-privilege secrets stored in internal repositories and secret scanning tools. Absence of secret scanning and open unresolved alerts allowed lateral movement across Uber's infrastructure.
detectionSignals: Secret scanning not enabled on repository
Open secret scanning alerts left unresolved
Long-lived credentials committed or accessible without rotation policy

name: event-stream npm package hijack
description: A malicious maintainer was granted ownership of the popular event-stream npm package and injected a dependency (flatmap-stream) containing obfuscated code designed to steal cryptocurrency wallet credentials from a specific downstream application. The attack exploited blind trust in transitive dependencies.
detectionSignals: Dependabot alerts not enabled on repository
Open critical or high severity dependency vulnerability alerts
No automated dependency update policy in place

name: SolarWinds Orion supply chain attack
description: Nation-state attackers compromised SolarWinds' build environment and injected the SUNBURST backdoor into signed Orion software updates. The attack went undetected for months because no code scanning or integrity verification was in place to detect the injected code.
detectionSignals: Code scanning not configured on repository
No recent code analysis runs
Build pipeline lacks integrity checks or reproducible build verification

name: GitHub Actions self-hosted runner crypto-mining abuse
description: Attackers discovered public repositories with self-hosted runners configured to execute workflows triggered by pull requests from forks. By submitting pull requests, they executed arbitrary code on self-hosted infrastructure to mine cryptocurrency and exfiltrate runner environment secrets.
detectionSignals: Self-hosted runner used in workflow triggered by pull_request on a public repository
Workflow triggered by pull_request_target runs on a self-hosted runner
Self-hosted runner labels do not restrict execution to trusted contexts

name: Trivy force-push to main
description: An attacker force-pushed directly to the main branch where branch protection controls were insufficient, bypassing expected review and integrity checks.
detectionSignals: Default branch protection does not block force pushes
Required pull request reviews are disabled on the default branch
No status checks required before merge

name: Praetorian self-hosted runner lateral movement
description: Red team obtained a PAT with repo scope, created a malicious workflow targeting org-wide self-hosted runners, escalated to root via Docker group membership, and pivoted into the internal network. Demonstrates why runners should be scoped to specific repos and why ephemeral runners matter.
detectionSignals: Org-wide runner groups available to all repositories
Self-hosted runners not configured as ephemeral
Runner groups not restricted to specific repositories or workflows
Self-hosted runners accessible from public repositories

name: prt-scan AI-automated PR poisoning
description: AI-assisted threat actor opened 475+ malicious PRs in 26 hours targeting repos with pull_request_target trigger. Approximately 10% success rate across 450+ attempts. High-value targets (Sentry, OpenSearch, NixOS) blocked it with first-time contributor approval gates and actor-restricted workflows. Six waves from six accounts traced to a single actor.
detectionSignals: Workflows using pull_request_target that checkout untrusted PR code
No first-time contributor approval gates for fork PRs
Secrets accessible in pull_request_target context
No actor-restriction conditions on sensitive workflows

name: hackerbot-claw autonomous CI/CD attacker
description: Autonomous bot used 5 different exploitation techniques across 7 successful high-profile attacks. Exfiltrated a GitHub token with write permissions from a highly popular repository. First known AI-powered CI/CD attacker preceding prt-scan.
detectionSignals: Workflows using pull_request_target with untrusted code checkout
Secrets accessible to fork PR workflows
No egress controls on CI runners
Write-permission tokens available in PR-triggered workflows

name: Trivy supply chain worm
description: Most sophisticated supply chain attack on a security tool to date. Combined credential theft, tag poisoning, binary tampering, persistent backdoors, and a self-propagating worm. 28 npm packages compromised in under 60 seconds. Originated from pull_request_target misconfiguration leading to credential theft.
detectionSignals: Pull_request_target trigger with untrusted code checkout
No egress controls to detect exfiltration
Actions referenced by mutable tags instead of SHA
No network egress filtering on CI runners

name: GitHub App installation token abuse
description: Pattern where overly permissive GitHub App installations at org level (especially with contents:write and actions:write) allow attackers who compromise any single app to modify workflows and trigger them across all repos the app has access to.
detectionSignals: GitHub Apps installed org-wide with write permissions
Apps with both contents:write and actions:write permissions
Apps with contents:write and workflows:write — direct workflow file injection path
Apps with secrets:write — can enumerate or overwrite repository secrets
Apps with packages:write — can publish malicious package versions to GitHub Package Registry
Stale or unused app installations widening attack surface
Apps with administration permission that can modify repo settings

name: XZ Utils (liblzma) maintainer backdoor
description: A long-running social engineering operation culminated in a malicious maintainer ("Jia Tan") gaining commit access to xz-utils and inserting an obfuscated backdoor into liblzma that compromised sshd on affected systems. The attack hinged on a single trusted maintainer with broad write access, no code-owner enforcement, and unsigned commits reaching downstream distributions.
detectionSignals: Repository has a single maintainer with unrestricted write access
CODEOWNERS absent or uses catch-all assigned to one user
Default branch does not require signed commits
Commits merged without secondary reviewer

name: Unauthorized deployment via unprotected environment
description: Generic pattern in which GitHub deployment environments are configured without required reviewers, wait timers, or deployment branch policies. Any workflow with access to the environment can push code to production without human approval, turning a compromised PR or workflow into a direct path to prod.
detectionSignals: Deployment environments without required reviewers
Environments with no wait timer for production deployments
No deployment branch policy restricting which refs can deploy
Environment secrets accessible to workflows without approval gates

name: Toyota source code public repository exposure
description: Toyota disclosed that portions of its T-Connect connected-car source code had been hosted on a public GitHub repository for roughly five years, exposing an access key for a customer data server. The root cause was a repository whose visibility did not match its sensitivity, with no visibility review or naming-convention audit.
detectionSignals: Public repositories with names suggesting internal or private use
No repository visibility review process
Secrets committed to public repositories
Repository visibility mismatched with org conventions

name: Azure Karpenter Provider Pwn Request
description: Pwn Request vulnerability exploited in Microsoft's open-source Azure Karpenter Provider project. StepSecurity Harden-Runner detected the attack in real-time and reported to MSRC within an hour. Could have compromised the cloud environment the project had access to.
detectionSignals: Pull_request_target trigger with checkout of untrusted PR code
No egress monitoring to detect exfiltration attempts
Secrets accessible in pull_request_target context
No harden-runner or equivalent egress control in place

name: Shai-Hulud npm worm
description: A self-propagating npm supply chain campaign that compromised maintainer publish tokens, pushed malicious versions of legitimate packages, and harvested additional credentials from CI environments. The package-registry phase relied on token-backed publishing without strong provenance controls.
detectionSignals: npm publish in CI without --provenance
Long-lived NPM_TOKEN or NODE_AUTH_TOKEN used for publishing
No OIDC trusted publishing relationship for npm
Publish pipeline changes without workflow path ownership controls

name: lottie-player npm compromise
description: Compromised maintainer npm credentials were used to publish malicious lottie-player versions containing wallet-drainer behavior. The incident demonstrated how token theft on a single maintainer account can cascade to hundreds of thousands of downstream websites.
detectionSignals: npm publish jobs rely on static publish tokens
No provenance emitted on npm releases
No environment approval gate for release workflows
Publishing from mutable branch pushes instead of controlled release refs

name: ua-parser-js npm compromise
description: The ua-parser-js maintainer account was compromised and malicious versions were published to npm with credential-stealing and cryptomining payloads. This incident highlighted that highly downloaded packages need hardened publish pipelines and short-lived credentials.
detectionSignals: Maintainer uses long-lived publish credentials
No trusted publishing via OIDC
No release provenance on published artifacts
No mandatory review controls for publish workflow changes

name: Bitwarden CLI npm compromise (2026-04)
description: An attacker compromised a maintainer account, modified the publish workflow, exchanged GitHub Actions OIDC for npm credentials via Trusted Publishing, and released a malicious Bitwarden CLI package with staged credential theft. The campaign targeted developer secrets, cloud tokens, and AI tool configuration files, then propagated through compromised GitHub tokens.
detectionSignals: Publish workflow has id-token: write without environment gating
Publish workflow triggered by push instead of release or tag
Workflow path changes are not protected by CODEOWNERS
Trusted publishing enabled but lacking human approval guardrails

name: Committed credentials exposure
description: Credentials committed to git history are permanent even after removal — git rm preserves secrets in the commit graph. Uber (2016) had AWS keys committed to a private repo accessed by attackers, breaching ~57M records. Toyota (2022) disclosed partner credentials committed in 2017 and publicly exposed for five years, affecting 296K customers. Ongoing solo-maintainer PAT leaks follow the same pattern. Once a secret lands in git, the only durable mitigations are rotation, secret scanning with push protection, and alert remediation.
detectionSignals: Secret scanning disabled on repository
Secret scanning enabled but unremediated High or Critical alerts open longer than 30 days
Push protection not enabled — allows secrets to be committed before detection
No secret rotation policy after a scanning alert fires

name: Dropbox GitHub breach
description: Dropbox disclosed that attackers phished employee credentials and accessed internal GitHub repositories. Mandatory MFA was highlighted as a key control to reduce credential-only account compromise impact.
detectionSignals: Organization does not require two-factor authentication for all members
Credential-only access to organization resources
No enforced MFA baseline for maintainers with write access

name: Gentoo GitHub organization compromise
description: The Gentoo GitHub organization was compromised and malicious changes were pushed to repositories. Broad access paths and weak organization-level access governance amplified blast radius.
detectionSignals: Organization default repository permission is write or admin
Overly broad baseline access across repositories
No least-privilege default access posture

name: GitHub OAuth device code phishing
description: Device authorization phishing abuses OAuth device flow by social-engineering users to approve attacker-generated device codes at github.com/login/device. Tokens can be minted with broad scopes if organization app restrictions are weak.
detectionSignals: Third-party OAuth app restrictions disabled
No organization controls for OAuth app authorization
Weak token governance and missing audit-log visibility for suspicious authorizations

name: GitHub Actions script injection via untrusted event context
description: A recurring GitHub Actions anti-pattern where attacker-controlled event fields (issue/PR/comment/discussion content, commit messages, wiki page names) are interpolated directly into run steps, enabling command injection in CI jobs.
detectionSignals: ${{ github.event.*.body }} or ${{ github.event.*.title }} expressions interpolated in run steps
run blocks consuming discussion/comment/review payloads without sanitization
Shell commands built from untrusted event data

name: Artifact poisoning across workflow_run boundaries
description: A low-privileged workflow uploads attacker-influenced artifacts that are later downloaded and executed by a higher-privileged workflow_run pipeline without integrity verification.
detectionSignals: actions/download-artifact combined with direct script execution
workflow_run trigger consuming artifacts from another workflow
No checksum, signature, or provenance verification before execution

name: OIDC trust abuse from unscoped token requests
description: Workflow requests id-token: write without strong trust scoping, enabling misuse of OIDC federation paths when repository or workflow trust boundaries are compromised.
detectionSignals: id-token: write without environment protection or reviewer gate
Trusted publishing jobs lacking environment scoping
Workflow OIDC grants broader than required refs/audiences

name: PR branch cache poisoning
description: Cache keys derived from attacker-controlled branch refs let adversaries poison build caches and influence subsequent trusted runs.
detectionSignals: Cache key includes github.head_ref or pull_request head ref
Shared cache restore keys across trusted and untrusted branches
Fork PR workflows reusing mutable cache namespaces

name: Shai-Hulud runner backdoor pattern
description: Attack chain pattern where weakly filtered discussion/comment triggers on self-hosted runners allow attacker-controlled execution and persistence on runner infrastructure, expanding the Shai-Hulud campaign techniques.
detectionSignals: Self-hosted runners exposed to discussion or issue_comment triggers
Trigger events without types filters in runner-executed workflows
Untrusted event payload interpolation in run steps on persistent runners
